text: Kahneman's most personal insight in Thinking Fast and Slow concerns the experiencing self and the remembering self — a distinction that cuts across both Systems. The experiencing self lives moment to moment, registering pain or pleasure in real time. The remembering self constructs a narrative afterward, weighting the peak and the ending heavily while ignoring duration entirely. This peak-end rule means a colonoscopy that ends on a less painful note is remembered as better than a shorter but more abruptly ending one, even if total discomfort was greater. We do not choose between experiences; we choose between memories of them.
question: What does the peak-end rule state about how the remembering self evaluates an experience?

options.text: It averages discomfort uniformly across the entire duration
It weights the peak and the ending heavily while largely ignoring duration
It prioritizes the beginning and the end of an experience equally
It relies on System 2 to reconstruct a full timeline of events
